title: Eccles cakes
subtitle: Flaky pastry with spiced currant filling
excerpt: Rough puff pastry rounds filled with currants, brown sugar, and mixed spice, baked until the pastry is deeply caramelised and the filling bubbles at the edges. Named after the Lancashire town. A small, dense, intensely flavoured pastry.
sourceNotes: Eccles cakes are documented from the town of Eccles in Lancashire from at least the late eighteenth century. A recipe appears in Elizabeth Raffald, The Experienced English Housekeeper (1769), under the name 'sweet patties', and the Eccles connection is established in published sources from the early nineteenth century. The round flaky pastry with currant filling has been manufactured continuously by bakeries in Eccles since the 1790s. The recipe is public domain and reproduced in countless British regional cookery collections.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked. Bake from frozen, adding 5 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Scale filling and pastry together. Bake in batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Filled, unbaked cakes can be refrigerated for up to 24 hours before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven; fan at 200°C. High heat is required for the pastry to puff and the surface to caramelise. The sugar on the top crust should amber, not just melt.

glossaryTerms.definition: The standard finish for Eccles cakes: three diagonal cuts through the dough to expose the dark filling beneath, then a brush of egg white and a scatter of caster sugar before baking. The slashes allow steam to escape and prevent the pastry blowing open unevenly; the sugar caramelises to a crackling crust.

content.text: Eccles cakes are less about technique and more about restraint with the filling: too much and the pastry bursts, too little and the balance is wrong. The currant filling should be slightly damp from the butter so it softens inside the pastry during baking. The
finish is not optional — the cuts allow the steam to vent and the sugar produces the characteristic crackling crust. Serve warm with Lancashire cheese.

Add the cold butter cubes to the flour and salt, coating them in flour without rubbing — the cubes should remain largely intact. Add the cold water and mix to a shaggy dough. On a floured surface, roll out into a long rectangle and fold into thirds. Rotate and fold again. Repeat for 4 folds total. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes.
Mix the currants, brown sugar, melted butter, mixed spice, and lemon zest until combined. The butter should coat the currants.

Preheat the oven to 220°C conventional (200°C fan). Roll the chilled pastry to 3 mm and cut out 12 circles approximately 12 cm in diameter. Place a heaped tablespoon of filling in the centre of each. Bring the pastry edges up around the filling and pinch together firmly to seal. Turn the cake over so the sealed side is on the bottom, and flatten gently with your palm to a round about 8 cm across. Place on lined trays sealed-side down.

Brush the tops with egg white and scatter caster sugar over. Cut three diagonal slits through each cake using a sharp knife — cut through to the filling, not just the surface. Bake for 18–22 minutes until the pastry is deep amber and the filling is bubbling through the slits. Cool on a rack for 10 minutes before eating; the filling is very hot.
